content: For a @MainActor ObservableObject with @Published properties, keep jobs collection directly on @MainActor — all @Published state for SwiftUI observation must be on @MainActor anyway. A dedicated actor introduces async hops: every state mutation requires await MainActor.run{}. For a single-user menu-bar app where all observable state is UI-facing, @Published var jobs on @MainActor AppState is simpler and avoids actor-hopping overhead. Use a dedicated actor only when background state does NOT need @Published propagation (e.g., caching layers, I/O coordination). The existing pattern in AppState — spawn Task { do { let r = try await work() ... mutate on MainActor } } — generalizes cleanly to N concurrent tasks.
